bibliographic_data.title: Observation of miniaturized bound states in the continuum with ultra-high quality factors
bibliographic_data.abstract: Light trapping is a constant pursuit in photonics because of its importance in science and technology. Many mechanisms have been explored, including the use of mirrors made of materials or structures that forbid outgoing waves 1–8, and bound states in the continuum that are mirror-less but based on topology 9–14. Here we report a compound method, combing mirrors and bound states in the continuum in an optimized way, to achieve a class of on-chip optical cavities that have high quality factors and small modal volumes. Speciﬁcally, light is trapped in the transverse direction by the photonic band gap of the lateral hetero-structure and conﬁned in the vertical direction by the constellation of multiple bound states in the continuum. As a result, unlike most bound states in the continuum found in photonic crystal slabs that are de-localized Bloch modes, we achieve light-trapping in all three dimensions and experimentally demonstrate quality factors as high as Q = 1.09 × 106 and modal volumes as low as V = 3.56 µm3 in the telecommunication regime.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a compound method combining mirrors and bound states in the continuum to achieve on-chip optical cavities with high quality factors and small modal volumes.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use a lateral hetero-structure to trap light in the transverse direction by the photonic band gap, while multiple bound states in the continuum confine it vertically. The method involves arranging topological charges in the momentum space of the photonic crystal slab to minimize out-of-plane radiation.
outcomes: Achieved high quality factors (Q = 1.09 × 106) and small modal volumes (V = 3.56 µm3)
Demonstrated robustness through statistical study of multiple fabricated devices
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper discusses the pursuit of light trapping in photonics, focusing on methods such as mirrors and bound states in the continuum (BICs). The authors propose a novel method combining these techniques to achieve high quality factors and small modal volumes. Specifically, they use a lateral hetero-structure to trap light transversely by the photonic band gap while BICs confine it vertically. Through careful design of topological charges in the momentum space, the out-of-plane radiation is minimized, leading to ultra-high Q values and reduced modal volumes. The method was experimentally validated with quality factors up to 1.09 × 106 and modal volumes as low as 3.56 µm³.
